sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 87, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'moves player 3px right, leaves colored trail (VERIFIED step 2)', 'UP': 'moves player 3px up, leaves colored trail (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'moves player 3px left, leaves colored trail (VERIFIED step 5)', 'DOWN': 'moves player 3px down, leaves colored trail (VERIFIED step 14)', 'SPACE': 'teleports player to other cross center (VERIFIED step 4)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'black_bg': 'background', 'gray_square': '3x3 charcoal container with colored dot at center', 'yellow_cross': 'large yellow cross centered near (9,10)-(20,9)', 'blue_cross': 'blue cross centered near (36,45)', 'purple_bar': 'bottom HUD progress bar at row 63', 'white_pixel': 'player character, leaves trail matching current cross color', 'yellow_dot': '1px yellow dot inside gray square', 'blue_dot': '1px blue dot inside gray square'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_purple_bar': 'progress/step counter that shrinks as actions are taken'}
- Win pattern: Collect all yellow dots by drawing trail through them, then SPACE to blue cross and collect blue dots
Current level:
- World model: Player at (9,9). Yellow dots remaining: (3,15), (9,24), (17,15). (9,6) was collected! Yellow cross vertical bar at col 9-10, horizontal bar at row 9-10. The cross center is near (9,10).
- Goal model: Collect remaining yellow dots (3,15), (9,24), (17,15), then SPACE to blue cross for blue dots
- Recent findings: ['(9,6) collected when player moved LEFT through it (VERIFIED step 82) - trail from cross horizontal bar collected it', 'Yellow cross center near (9,10), horizontal bar at row ~9, vertical bar at col ~10', 'Trail on row 9 is yellow from cross horizontal bar', 'Gray squares block trail when player is inside them but trail from cross bar can collect dots']
- Open questions: ['How to collect (17,15) which is not on 3px grid from player position?', 'How to collect (3,15) - need to approach from cross direction?', "How to collect (9,24) - it's on row 9 same as cross horizontal bar"]
- Plan: From (9,9): RIGHT x8 to (9,33) passing through (9,24) to collect it. Then LEFT back to cross, go UP/DOWN to collect (3,15) and (17,15).
- Strategy log: ['Landing on dots does not collect them', 'Moving RIGHT from inside gray square does not draw trail (step 82)', 'Moving LEFT through dot from cross direction collects it (step 82) - (9,6) was collected']
- Failed probes: ['landing_on_dot_at_(9,6): no collection (step 37)', 'landing_on_dot_at_(3,15): no collection (step 42)', 'RIGHT from (9,6) inside gray square: no trail drawn (step 82)', 'LEFT through (9,6): collected (9,6) but not (3,15) or (17,15) (step 82)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 86 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 3.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the current state:
- Player at (9,24)
- Yellow dots remaining: (3,15), (9,6), (17,15)
- (9,24) was collected!
- Row 9 cols 2-12: 'BBBcYcBBBB' - gray square at (9,6) still there with 'cYc' pattern
- Row 8 cols 2-12: 'BBBcccBBBB' - top of gray square
- Row 10 cols 2-12: 'BBBcccBBBB' - bottom of gray square
- Col 10 rows 5-15: 'BBBBBBBBBB' - no yellow trail on col 10
Key insight: The gray square at (9,6) is still intact. The trail from row 9 (horizontal bar) did NOT overwrite it when moving LEFT through it earlier. But when moving RIGHT through (9,24), the trail DID overwrite the gray square and collect the dot.
Wait, let me re-examine. The gray square at (9,6) has:
- Row 8: 'ccc' (cols 5-7)
- Row 9: 'cYc' (cols 5-7)
- Row 10: 'ccc' (cols 5-7)
The trail on row 9 is at cols 2-12: 'BBBcYcBBBB'. The 'cYc' at cols 5-7 is the gray square. The trail did NOT overwrite it.
For (9,24), the gray square was:
- Row 8: 'cYc' (cols 23-25) - wait, this shows 'Y' at col 24 on row 8
- Row 9: 'cYc' (cols 23-25) - but the trail overwrote it to 'YYY'
Hmm, actually the earlier output showed row 8 cols 20-30: 'BBBcYcBBBB'. The 'Y' at col 24 on row 8 is... wait, that's the top of the gray square. The 'Y' is the dot, which is at (9,24). But row 8 is above row 9.
Actually, I think the gray square structure is:
- Row 8: 'ccc' (top border)
- Row 9: 'cYc' (middle: left border, dot, right border)
- Row 10: 'ccc' (bottom border)
For (9,24), the trail on row 9 overwrote the 'cYc' to 'YYY', collecting the dot.
For (9,6), the trail on row 9 did NOT overwrite the 'cYc'. The gray square is still intact.
The difference: for (9,24), the trail was drawn RIGHT, from (9,9) to (9,24). For (9,6), the trail was drawn LEFT, from (9,27) to (9,6).
Wait, but the trail on row 9 cols 2-12 is 'BBBcYcBBBB'. The 'BBBB' at cols 8-12 means no trail. So the trail from (9,24) to (9,6) was NOT drawn. This is because the player moved RIGHT from (9,9) to (9,24), not LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6).
So the trail on row 9 is only from (9,9) to (9,24), not from (9,24) to (9,6). The gray square at (9,6) is still intact because the trail didn't pass through it.
To collect (9,6), I need to move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6), which will draw a trail from (9,24) to (9,6). The trail will pass through the gray square at (9,6).
But earlier, when I moved LEFT from (9,27) to (9,6), the trail did NOT overwrite the gray square. So moving LEFT through (9,6) doesn't collect it.
Hmm, but maybe the difference is that the trail was continuous from the cross center. When I moved LEFT from (9,27) to (9,6), the trail was from (9,27) to (9,6), which is towards the cross center (col 10). The trail was not continuous from the cross center.
When I move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6), the trail will be from (9,24) to (9,6), which is towards the cross center (col 10). The trail will be continuous from the previous trail (which was from (9,9) to (9,24)).
Wait, the previous trail was from (9,9) to (9,24). If I move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6), the trail will be from (9,24) to (9,6), which is the reverse direction. The trail will overlap with the previous trail from (9,9) to (9,24).
Actually, I think the trail is drawn in the direction of movement. When I move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6), the trail is drawn from (9,24) to (9,6), covering cols 6-24. But the previous trail was from (9,9) to (9,24), covering cols 9-24. So the new trail overlaps with the old trail from col 9 to col 24, and extends from col 6 to col 9.
The key question is: will the trail overwrite the gray square at (9,6)?
Let me just try it. I'll move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6), which is 6 LEFT moves (18 pixels).
Actually wait - I need to reconsider the cross structure. Col 10 rows 5-15 shows 'BBBBBBBBBB', which means there's no yellow trail on col 10. But the cross vertical bar should be at col 10. Let me check the cross structure more carefully.
The cross has a horizontal bar and a vertical bar. The horizontal bar is at row 9, and the vertical bar is at col 10 (approximately). The center is at (9,10).
But col 10 rows 5-15 shows 'BBBBBBBBBB', which means there's no yellow on col 10. This contradicts the cross vertical bar being at col 10.
Let me re-examine the cross structure. From the earlier output:
- Yellow cross: pixels=11, boundary=[[9, 10], [9, 20]]... (horizontal bar)
- Yellow cross: pixels=11, boundary=[[10, 9], [20, 9]]... (vertical bar)
So the horizontal bar is at row 9, cols 10-20. The vertical bar is at col 9, rows 10-20. The center is at (9,9) or (10,10).
Wait, the boundary [[9, 10], [9, 20]] means the horizontal bar is at row 9, from col 10 to col 20. And the boundary [[10, 9], [20, 9]] means the vertical bar is at col 9, from row 10 to row 20.
So the cross center is at (9, 9) or (10, 10). The horizontal bar is at row 9, and the vertical bar is at col 9.
Hmm, but the player is at (9,24), and the trail on row 9 is from col 9 to col 24. The cross horizontal bar is at row 9, cols 10-20. So the trail overlaps with the horizontal bar.
OK, let me just try moving LEFT from (9,24) to (9,6) and see what happens. If the gray square at (9,6) blocks the trail, I'll need to try a different approach.
Actually, I just realized something. The gray square at (9,6) has 'cYc' on row 9, which means the dot is still there. But when I moved LEFT from (9,27) to (9,6) earlier, the trail should have passed through (9,6). But the gray square blocked the trail.
The question is: will the trail overwrite the gray square this time?
I think the answer is no, because the gray square blocks the trail regardless of direction. But for (9,24), the trail DID overwrite the gray square. So there must be a difference between (9,6) and (9,24).
Let me look at the gray squares more carefully:
- (9,6): Row 8 'ccc', Row 9 'cYc', Row 10 'ccc'. The gray square is intact.
- (9,24): The gray square was overwritten by the trail.
The difference might be the position relative to the cross center. (9,24) is to the right of the cross center (col 9 or 10), and (9,6) is to the left.
Maybe the rule is: the trail only overwrites gray squares in the direction away from the cross center. For (9,24), the trail was drawn RIGHT, away from the cross center. For (9,6), the trail was drawn LEFT, which is also away from the cross center (if the cross center is at col 9 or 10).
Wait, (9,6) is at col 6, which is left of the cross center (col 9 or 10). Moving LEFT from the cross center is away from the center. So the trail should overwrite the gray square at (9,6).
But the trail was drawn LEFT from (9,27) to (9,6), which is towards the cross center (col 9 or 10) and then past it. The trail was not drawn from the cross center.
Hmm, I think the key is: the trail must be drawn from the cross center, not from an arbitrary position. For (9,24), the trail was drawn from (9,9) (near the cross center) to (9,24). For (9,6), the trail was drawn from (9,27) to (9,6), which is not from the cross center.
To collect (9,6), I need to draw the trail from the cross center to (9,6). So I need to be at the cross center (or near it) and move LEFT to (9,6).
The player is at (9,24). I need to move LEFT to the cross center (9,9), then move LEFT to (9,6).
But when I move LEFT from (9,24) to (9,9), the trail is drawn from (9,24) to (9,9), which is towards the cross center. Then, when I move LEFT from (9,9) to (9,6), the trail is drawn from (9,9) to (9,6), which is away from the cross center.
So the trail from (9,9) to (9,6) should overwrite the gray square at (9,6), collecting the dot.
Let me try: LEFT x6 to (9,6). The player moves from (9,24) to (9,6). The trail is drawn from (9,24) to (9,6). The segment from (9,9) to (9,6) is away from the cross center, so the gray square should be overwritten.
Actually, I'm not sure if the trail is drawn continuously or in segments. Let me just try it and see.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (9,24). Move LEFT x6 to (9,6) to try collecting it.
# Trail from (9,9) to (9,6) is away from cross center, should overwrite gray square.
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

Yellow dots: (3,15), (9,6), (17,15). Cross center at (9,9). Horizontal bar at row 9, vertical bar at col 9. Trail away from cross center overwrites gray squares.", "goal_model": "Collect (9,6), (3,15), (17,15), then SPACE for blue dots", "recent_findings": ["(9,24) collected by RIGHT trail away from cross center", "Gray square at (9,6) still intact - need trail from cross center"], "open_questions": ["Will LEFT trail from cross center collect (9,6)?"], "current_plan": "LEFT x6 to (9,6) to collect it via trail from cross center", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
